text: This video is intended for patients hoping to learn more about their upcoming surgery. Viewer discretion is advised.
The video shows a large cyst on the liver overlying the kidney, visibly compressing and deforming the affected kidney, occupying much of the working space in the upper abdomen.
The camera pans to reveal a tumor located in the upper aspect of the kidney, positioned near the hilum where the renal arteries and veins enter and exit the kidney.

The video shows two renal arteries originating from the aorta and extending towards the kidney, along with two main renal veins, which are visible and will be addressed during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the nephrectomy procedure by excising the large liver cyst that overlies the kidney, creating additional working space in the upper abdomen.
A retractor is placed under the liver to elevate it, providing access to the kidney located beneath.
The surgeon incises the fascia overlying the kidney, revealing the normal fat and connective tissue encasing the kidney, which will need to be opened for further access.
The surgeon utilizes the fourth arm of the robotic system to assist with retraction, enhancing the exposure of the kidney and surrounding structures.
After removing the initial layers of fascia, the surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava, followed by the visualization of the patient's two renal veins.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein as it drains into the inferior vena cava, using it as a landmark to dissect downward and expose the ureter located behind the vein.
The surgeon observes the ureter intermittently contracting and elevates it to reveal the anatomical plane beneath the kidney and above the psoas muscle.
The surgeon cauterizes and divides superficial veins to expose the lower renal artery, revealing the artery beneath the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the lower pole renal artery, preparing it for clamping to limit blood loss during the tumor resection.
The surgeon places a vessel loop around the renal artery, providing gentle retraction to facilitate further dissection and exposure.
The surgeon uses a stapler to safely ligate the lower renal vein, which overlies the main renal artery, while ensuring the upper vein remains intact to preserve kidney function.
The surgeon identifies the main renal artery located behind the upper renal vein and carefully dissects it to allow for clamp placement during tumor resection.
After dissecting the renal hilum, the surgeon opens the connective tissue over the kidney and removes the surrounding fat to visually expose the tumor.
The surgeon retracts the overlying fat upwards, revealing the kidney capsule beneath it.

The surgeon completes the exposure process, revealing the junction between the tumor and the normal kidney tissue, as well as the tumor's proximity to the renal hilum. The planned margin of resection around the tumor is marked with cautery on the kidney capsule.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound with picture-in-picture capability to verify the depth of the planned resection margin, ensuring it is sufficient to avoid cutting into the tumor. The tumor is clearly visible and prepared for resection.
The surgeon pre-places sutures around the kidney to prepare for closing the defect after the tumor is resected, ensuring the sutures are positioned for effective closure post-resection.
The surgeon pre-places clamps on the arteries supplying the kidney, preparing for vascular control during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon clamps the arteries supplying the kidney to prevent bleeding during tumor removal, aiming to minimize the duration of ischemia to preserve normal kidney function.

The surgeon injects a fluorescent dye called ICG and activates a special laser light, revealing areas with blood flow as green. The kidney and tumor remain unlit, confirming successful clamping of blood flow and readiness for tumor resection.

The surgeon begins resecting the tumor from the kidney after placing clamps on the renal artery, carefully excising a small margin of normal kidney tissue beneath the tumor to ensure complete removal.
The surgeon completes the excision of the tumor, setting it aside for later retrieval. The defect in the kidney is then closed by suturing the small veins and arteries at the base of the defect.
The surgeon closes the defect in the kidney by placing sutures across the hole and using clips to apply gentle pressure, effectively preventing bleeding from the wound.

Following the closure of the surgical site, the surgeon removes the clamps from the renal artery, allowing blood to flow back into the kidney. The surgical site shows no signs of bleeding. Additional clips are applied to each suture for enhanced security.
The excised tumor is placed into a retrieval bag, and products that promote clotting are applied over the surgical field to reduce venous oozing.
A surgical drain is placed over the kidney to manage potential bleeding or urine leakage. The tumor is extracted, and the incision sites are closed, marking the completion of the procedure.
